title: RevisionBench: A Compact Benchmark for Abstract-Local Scientific Claim Updates
description: RevisionBench is a compact benchmark built from scientific abstract revisions that asks whether models can reject an obsolete earlier-version claim in favor of the current claim using only the latest abstract as local evidence. Its novelty claim is deliberately narrow: adjudicated obsolete-vs-current scientific claim updates from real revision histories, not revision data or evolving-knowledge evaluation in general.
motivation: Recent 2024-2025 benchmarks such as EvoWiki, HoH, EvolveBench, ChronoFact, and evolveQA already cover evolving knowledge, temporal awareness, stale retrieval, or temporal fact verification, while arXivEdits, CASIMIR, and VitaminC show that revision histories can already be aligned and repurposed. The remaining gap is a carefully validated scientific benchmark for abstract-local claim updates, which matters because literature assistants and scientific verification systems can easily repeat stale claims from earlier paper versions.
proposed_approach: Mine edited abstract sentence pairs from multi-version arXiv papers, run conservative lexical alignment plus a lightweight paraphrase-aware recovery pass, and annotate candidates with a two-stage protocol over earlier sentence, later sentence, earlier abstract, and latest abstract. Release a benchmark-first primary task for current-claim selection, add a symmetric pairwise NLI baseline that scores both claims against the same latest abstract without privileging the later claim, and retain the secondary obsolete/supported_current/insufficient_evidence task only if a preregistered pilot clears agreement thresholds.

related_work.title: arXivEdits: Understanding the Human Revision Process in Scientific Writing
related_work.authors: Chao Jiang, Wei Xu, Samuel Stevens
related_work.difference: arXivEdits is the closest scientific revision precursor, but it is a revision-process corpus rather than a benchmark for obsolete-vs-current scientific claim discrimination.

related_work.title: CASIMIR: A Corpus of Scientific Articles Enhanced with Multiple Author-Integrated Revisions
related_work.authors: Leane Jourdan, Florian Boudin, Nicolas Hernandez, Richard Dufour
related_work.difference: CASIMIR scales scientific revision alignment and weakens any novelty claim based on revision histories themselves; RevisionBench differs only at the task level by keeping adjudicated factual-status changes and evaluating held-out claims.

related_work.title: Get Your Vitamin C! Robust Fact Verification with Contrastive Evidence
related_work.authors: Tal Schuster, Adam Fisch, Regina Barzilay
related_work.difference: VitaminC already shows that document revisions can be turned into fact-verification data, so RevisionBench does not claim novelty for revisions-as-supervision; it narrows the setting to scientific abstract updates with local evidence.

related_work.title: EvoWiki: Evaluating LLMs on Evolving Knowledge
related_work.difference: EvoWiki studies evolving Wikipedia knowledge, whereas RevisionBench targets authored scientific claims that change within a single paper's revision chain.

related_work.title: HoH: A Dynamic Benchmark for Evaluating the Impact of Outdated Information on Retrieval-Augmented Generation
related_work.difference: HoH focuses on stale information in RAG pipelines, not on claim discrimination under fixed local abstract evidence.

related_work.title: EvolveBench: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Assessing Temporal Awareness in LLMs on Evolving Knowledge
related_work.difference: EvolveBench evaluates broad temporal awareness, while RevisionBench deliberately restricts itself to a narrow revision-aware scientific benchmark.

related_work.title: ChronoFact: Timeline-based Temporal Fact Verification
related_work.authors: Anab Maulana Barik, Wynne Hsu, Mong Li Lee
related_work.difference: ChronoFact studies temporal fact verification with external event timelines; RevisionBench instead uses paired authored revisions and the latest abstract as the sole evidence.

related_work.title: When Facts Change: Probing LLMs on Evolving Knowledge with evolveQA
related_work.difference: evolveQA probes timestamped evolving external knowledge, whereas RevisionBench isolates intra-document scientific claim change.

related_work.title: SciVer: Evaluating Foundation Models for Multimodal Scientific Claim Verification
related_work.authors: Chengye Wang, Yifei Shen, Zexi Kuang, Arman Cohan, Yilun Zhao
related_work.difference: SciVer is the closest static scientific claim-verification benchmark, but it does not test whether a once-plausible scientific claim has become obsolete across revisions.

hypothesis: If benchmark construction is restricted to adjudicated abstract-local claim updates, then strong open models will still underperform human agreement on selecting the current scientific claim, and semantic updates such as softening, retraction, or scope changes will be harder than simple local numeric updates.

confirm: A preregistered pilot over 120 candidates supports, under Wilson intervals, a realistic path to at least 150 retained Task 1 items from 700 to 900 annotated candidates.
The annotation process remains operationally feasible within roughly 58 to 79 annotator-hours including calibration and adjudication.
Agreement on substantiveness is substantial, and the obsolete-vs-insufficient_evidence distinction reaches at least 0.60 Cohen's kappa and 80% raw agreement before Task 2 is retained.
The symmetric pairwise NLI baseline is competitive but does not saturate the benchmark, showing the task is not solved by simply preferring the later claim.
Providing the latest abstract improves current-claim selection over claim-pair-only input with a paired bootstrap confidence interval excluding zero.
The benchmark retains at least 30 semantic-update items for coarse-grained analysis, or the final paper explicitly narrows its claim to a mostly local-update benchmark.
refute: The pilot shows that abstract-local obsolete cases are too rare to reach a credible benchmark size within the annotation budget.
Annotators cannot reliably distinguish obsolete from insufficient_evidence using only the paired sentences and abstracts.
The retained set collapses almost entirely to numeric edits, leaving too little semantic-update coverage for meaningful analysis.
Simple lexical, embedding, or symmetric NLI baselines reach annotator-level performance, leaving limited diagnostic value.
Adding the latest abstract provides no statistically meaningful gain over claim-pair-only evaluation, suggesting the task does not truly capture revision-aware reasoning.
